title: The Glory of the LORD Returns

sections.header: Verses 1-12 — The Glory Returns Through the East Gate

heb.paragraph: The return of the kavod forms the structural and theological climax of the entire book. The glory that departed through the east gate in stages (9:3; 10:18-19; 11:22-23) now re-enters through the same gate. The Hebrew noun kavod derives from the root kbd ('heavy, weighty') and denotes the visible, luminous manifestation of YHWH's presence. The departure-return trajectory constitutes the central narrative arc of Ezekiel: chapters 1-11 describe the glory's departure, chapters 12-39 address the reasons for and consequences of that departure, and chapters 40-48 narrate its restoration.

The phrase torat habayit (v. 12) provides the programmatic label for the entire vision of chapters 40-48. The word torah here functions not in its later sense of 'Mosaic law' but in its original priestly sense of 'instruction, direction' pertaining to sacred space. The 'law' is spatial holiness: the entire mountaintop is 'most holy' (qodesh qodashim), establishing concentric zones of sanctity radiating from the Most Holy Place outward.

panels.ctx: The return of YHWH's glory to the new temple constitutes the theological climax of the book of Ezekiel. The prophet is brought to the east gate, where he witnesses the kavod approaching from the east with a sound like many waters and a radiance that illuminates the earth. This reverses the departure sequence narrated in chapters 10-11. The divine voice from within the temple declares this as the place of YHWH's throne and the soles of his feet, where he will dwell among Israel forever. The condition for permanent divine presence is the removal of all defilement, specifically the corpses of kings (likely referring to royal tombs adjacent to the pre-exilic temple) and the idolatrous practices that desecrated the first temple. Verse 12 establishes the foundational principle of the entire temple vision: the law of the temple is absolute holiness on every side.

cross.note: The glory of the LORD departed from the threshold of the temple and stood over the cherubim, then moved to the east gate. Chapter 43 reverses this exact trajectory.
The glory ascended from within the city and stopped above the mountain east of it. The glory returns from this same eastern direction in 43:2.
At Solomon's temple dedication, the glory cloud filled the house so that the priests could not stand to minister. Ezekiel's vision promises an even more permanent divine indwelling.
The voice from the throne declares that God's dwelling is with humanity. Revelation's vision of ultimate divine presence draws heavily on Ezekiel's temple imagery.

notes.note: The return of God's glory to the temple is the most important event in Ezekiel's vision. Without the glory, the temple is merely an empty building. The glory had departed in stages (8-11), and its return validates the entire restoration program. This is the eschatological fulfillment that awaits Israel in the millennial kingdom.

God declares this temple as the place of His throne forever. The condition is holiness: Israel must put away spiritual adultery and the desecration of placing royal tombs near sacred space. The close proximity of the palace complex to the pre-exilic temple had blurred the distinction between the sacred and the profane.

Ezekiel is told to describe the temple to the house of Israel so that they may be ashamed of their iniquities. The detailed measurements serve a moral purpose: confronting Israel with the holiness they had violated. The law of the temple is total holiness on every side.

The sound of God's coming is compared to the roar of many waters, the same description used in 1:24 for the sound of the cherubim's wings. This deliberate echo confirms that the glory Ezekiel saw by the Kebar Canal is the same glory now returning.

The phrase 'soles of my feet' is anthropomorphic language indicating permanent, settled presence. The ark served as God's footstool (1 Chr 28:2), but in Ezekiel's temple no ark is mentioned because God Himself fills the space directly.

Calvin interprets the return of the glory not as a literal future event but as a figure for the restoration of the church after the exile. The glory represents God's gracious presence among His people, which was withdrawn due to their sin and is now promised in renewed form. For Calvin, this passage speaks ultimately of Christ, in whom the fullness of deity dwells bodily.

The command to describe the temple aims at producing repentance. Calvin stresses that the meticulous measurements are not architectural blueprints but moral standards. The temple's perfection shames Israel's imperfection. The 'law of the temple' — holiness on every side — is the abiding principle of the church in every age.

The phrase 'from the east' (midderekh haqqadim) uses the same directional marker as the glory's departure in 11:23, creating an inclusio. The NET notes that the sound like 'many waters' (rabbim) echoes both the initial vision (1:24) and anticipates later biblical theophanies (cf. Rev 1:15).

The Hebrew text reads 'the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet.' The NET notes that the absence of any reference to the ark of the covenant is striking and theologically significant: God's presence is unmediated by cultic furniture in this vision.

The phrase 'most holy' (qodesh qodashim) applies here to the entire mountaintop, not merely the inner sanctuary. This is an unprecedented expansion of the zone of highest sanctity, suggesting a radical reordering of sacred geography.

Block identifies this passage as the structural counterpart to 1:1-28. The book opened with the glory coming to Ezekiel in exile; it approaches its climax with the glory returning to its proper home. Block notes that the phrase 'like the vision I had seen' (v. 3) explicitly links this theophany to both the Kebar vision (ch. 1) and the departure vision (chs. 8-11), creating a three-point theological trajectory: revelation, departure, return.

Block emphasizes the motif of corpses of kings (pigrē malkhēhem). The pre-exilic temple and royal palace shared a common wall (the 'threshold beside my threshold,' v. 8), and royal burials within the palace complex defiled sacred space. The new temple eliminates this contamination by architectural design, not merely moral exhortation.

Zimmerli classifies this passage as a Gotteswort (divine word) embedded within a visionary narrative. The prophet functions as a passive witness; the divine kabod acts as the sole agent. Zimmerli notes that the tradition-historical background is the priestly theology of P, where the kabod filled the tabernacle (Exod 40:34-35). Ezekiel adapts this tradition eschatologically.

Zimmerli identifies the condemnation of royal tombs and the 'threshold beside my threshold' (v. 8) as targeting the Solomonic temple complex specifically. The torat habayit of v. 12 functions as a colophon for the entire vision: all that follows in chapters 44-48 are applications of this one principle of comprehensive holiness.

sections.header: Verses 13-17 — The Dimensions of the Altar

heb.paragraph: The base of the altar is designated cheiq ha-arets, literally 'bosom of the earth.' This unusual term establishes the altar as rooted in the ground itself, connecting sacrifice to creation. The altar rises in four stages: the cheiq (base trench), the azarah (lower ledge), the azarah (upper ledge), and the har-el / ariel (altar hearth). The total height is approximately 5.2 meters (11 cubits from ground to hearth).

The altar hearth bears two related names: har'el ('mountain of God,' v. 15) and ari'el ('hearth of God' or 'lion of God,' v. 15b-16). The wordplay is deliberate. The altar is simultaneously a miniature sacred mountain (har) and the place where sacrificial fire burns (ari'el). Isaiah 29:1 uses ariel as a name for Jerusalem itself. The four horns (qeranot) at the corners reflect ancient Near Eastern altar design and symbolize the altar's cosmic orientation to the four cardinal directions.

panels.ctx: The altar specifications follow immediately after the return of the glory, establishing the instrument of atonement as the first item of cultic furniture described. The altar rises in four stepped tiers from a base trench to the hearth, with each stage slightly smaller than the one below, creating a ziggurat-like profile. The measurements use the long cubit (approximately 52 cm), consistent with the measuring rod described in 40:5. Steps on the east side provide priestly access. The altar is oriented with its steps facing east, so that the officiating priest faces west toward the Most Holy Place, reversing the idolatrous orientation condemned in 8:16 where twenty-five men faced east to worship the sun.

cross.note: The tabernacle altar of burnt offering. Ezekiel's altar is considerably larger and more elaborate, reflecting the enhanced sanctity of the eschatological temple.
Isaiah uses 'Ariel' as a name for Jerusalem, employing the same term Ezekiel uses for the altar hearth. Both texts connect the altar with the identity of the city itself.

notes.note: The altar measurements establish a massive structure far larger than anything in the tabernacle or Solomonic temple. Its four-tiered design recalls ancient Near Eastern cosmic mountain symbolism, but repurposed for the worship of the true God. Each tier narrows as it ascends, directing the eye upward.

The hearth is a perfect square of twelve cubits, oriented to the four compass points by its horns. The square shape signifies completeness and order. The twelve-cubit dimension may echo the twelve tribes of Israel, whose offerings will be consumed upon it.

The steps face east, deliberately opposing the solar worship of 8:16. The priest ascends facing west, toward the Most Holy Place and YHWH's throne. Even the physical orientation of worship is theologically significant.

The 'long cubit' (cubit and a handbreadth) is approximately 20.5 inches, about three inches longer than the standard cubit. This ensures that every measurement in Ezekiel's temple vision is on a grander scale.

Calvin views the altar dimensions as expressing the sufficiency and grandeur of Christ's atoning work. The massive scale exceeds any historical altar because Christ's sacrifice exceeds all Levitical offerings. The stepped tiers signify the progressive revelation of redemption, culminating in the hearth where fire consumes the offering.

The NET notes the wordplay between har'el ('mountain of God') and ari'el ('hearth of God'). The Masoretic pointing distinguishes the two forms, but they likely represent variant spellings of the same concept: the altar as a sacred mountain in miniature.

The long cubit (ammah we-tophach) adds a handbreadth (approximately 7.4 cm) to the standard cubit. The NET observes this is the same measurement system used throughout chapters 40-48, distinct from the shorter cubit used in Solomon's temple description.

Block traces the altar design to Mesopotamian temple-tower (ziggurat) architecture, noting the stepped profile and cosmic mountain symbolism. However, he emphasizes that Ezekiel has thoroughly Yahwized the form: the altar serves YHWH alone, the four horns orient it to creation, and the east-facing steps correct the solar worship condemned in chapter 8. The altar is the point where heaven and earth meet.

Zimmerli identifies this unit as a priestly technical specification (Massliste) comparable to the altar descriptions in Exodus 27 and 2 Chronicles 4. He notes that the four-tiered design has no parallel in known Israelite altars and likely reflects a theological ideal rather than a historical prototype. The har'el/ari'el wordplay connects the altar to both cosmic mountain theology and the fire-theology of the cult.

heb.paragraph: The verb kipper occurs four times in this passage (vv. 20, 22, 26), establishing atonement as the altar's essential function. The root likely derives from Akkadian kuppuru ('to wipe, purge'). The seven-day consecration ritual purges the altar of any contamination so that it may function as the meeting point between holy God and sinful humanity. The parallel with the Mosaic tabernacle consecration (Exod 29; Lev 8-9) is deliberate: Ezekiel's new order requires a new inauguration.

A male goat is offered as a sin offering (chattat) each day for seven days, alongside a bull and a ram. The chattat ritual applies sacrificial blood to the altar's horns and corners to remove impurity. The seven-day duration follows the pattern of Leviticus 8-9 and underscores the completeness (sheva = seven) of the purification process.

panels.ctx: The seven-day altar consecration ritual mirrors the tabernacle inauguration of Exodus 29 and Leviticus 8-9, signaling that the new temple represents a new covenantal beginning. Each day requires a young bull as a sin offering, a male goat as a sin offering, and a ram and bull as burnt offerings, all without defect. The Zadokite priests (specified here for the first time in the vision) officiate, sprinkling blood on the altar horns and four corners, then burning the sin offering outside the sacred precinct. Salt accompanies the burnt offerings, echoing the covenant of salt (Lev 2:13; Num 18:19). After the seven days of purification, regular offerings commence on the eighth day, and YHWH declares that He will accept the people. The literary structure thus moves from glory-return (vv. 1-12) through altar-construction (vv. 13-17) to altar-consecration (vv. 18-27), establishing the complete restoration of sacrificial worship.

cross.note: The tabernacle altar consecration also lasted seven days, with daily sin offerings to purge the altar. Ezekiel's ritual follows the same pattern but with expanded sacrificial requirements.

The covenant of salt: every grain offering was seasoned with salt. Ezekiel 43:24 extends this requirement to the burnt offerings of the consecration ritual, reinforcing the covenantal significance of the altar.

The author of Hebrews argues that the heavenly tabernacle required better sacrifices than the earthly copies. Ezekiel's elaborate purification ritual foreshadows the ultimate cleansing accomplished by Christ's blood.

notes.note: The altar must be purged before it can function. Sin contaminates even sacred objects. The blood of the sin offering applied to the altar's horns and corners cleanses it from any ritual impurity. The burning of the sin offering outside the sacred precinct parallels the Day of Atonement ritual.

Salt on the burnt offerings points to the permanence of God's covenant (Num 18:19; 2 Chr 13:5). The offerings are salted before being presented, signifying that this is a covenant of perpetual obligation and divine faithfulness.

Seven days of consecration followed by acceptance on the eighth day. The eighth day marks new beginnings in biblical typology (circumcision on the eighth day, resurrection on the first day of a new week). God's acceptance of the people depends on proper consecration of the place of sacrifice.

The Zadokite priests are distinguished from the broader Levitical priesthood. Only they may approach YHWH to minister, because they remained faithful when the rest of Israel and even other Levites went astray (44:15). Faithfulness determines priestly privilege.

Calvin reads the entire consecration sequence as a type of Christ's priestly work. The seven days represent the fullness of time; the eighth day of acceptance represents the new creation inaugurated by the resurrection. The elaborate ritual demonstrates that no human approach to God is possible without atonement. Calvin cautions against drawing literal architectural conclusions from visionary material.

The salt covenant signifies the incorruptible nature of God's promises. Calvin connects this to Mark 9:49 ('everyone will be salted with fire'), interpreting both passages as pointing to the purifying and preserving work of the Holy Spirit in believers.

The NET notes that 'the Levitical priests who are of the descendants of Zadok' represents a restrictive identification. Not all Levites may serve at the altar; only the Zadokite line retains full priestly prerogatives. This anticipates the fuller discussion in chapter 44.

The application of blood to the four horns and four corners of the ledge 'purges' (kipper) the altar. The NET distinguishes this purgation offering (chattat) from expiatory sacrifice: the blood removes contamination from the sacred object rather than atoning for personal sin.

Block identifies this passage as a ritual ordination text (Weiheritual) with close parallels to P's consecration narratives. He notes three key innovations: (1) the Zadokite restriction on priestly office, (2) the expanded role of the male goat as a daily sin offering, and (3) the declaration of divine acceptance on the eighth day, which transforms the ritual from mere inauguration into a covenantal event.

Block highlights the phrase 'salt them with salt' as deliberately evoking the covenant of salt (berit melach). Salt's preservative quality makes it an apt symbol for covenant permanence. The inclusion of salt in burnt offerings is unusual and signals the theological weight of this consecration.

Zimmerli treats this section as a distinct literary unit within the temple torah, classifying it as a Ritualordnung (ritual ordinance). He notes that the seven-day schema reflects the priestly creation theology of P, where the seventh day completes a cycle. The eighth day of acceptance introduces an eschatological surplus: the new order surpasses the old. Zimmerli sees evidence of editorial layering, with the Zadokite specification in v. 19 possibly reflecting a later redactional addition to an originally more inclusive priestly text.

themes.note: The dominant themes are Holiness and Worship. The return of the glory and the altar consecration together establish that holiness is the precondition for worship and worship is the purpose of the temple. Sovereignty scores high because the glory's return is entirely YHWH's initiative.

lit.note: Chapter 43 moves from theophany (glory return) through cultic infrastructure (altar) to ritual inauguration (consecration), establishing the complete framework for restored worship.

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: The return of YHWH's glory to the new temple constitutes the theological climax of the book of Ezekiel…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Glory of the LORD Returns

translations.text: The vision I saw was like the vision I had seen when he came to destroy the city and like the visions I had seen by the Kebar River, and I fell facedown.
And the vision I saw was just like the vision that I had seen when he came to destroy the city, and just like the vision that I had seen by the Chebar canal. And I fell on my face.

He said: “Son of man, this is the place of my throne and the place for the soles of my feet. This is where I will live among the Israelites forever. The people of Israel will never again defile my holy name—neither they nor their kings—by their prostitution and the funeral offerings for their kings at their death.

and he said to me, “Son of man, this is the place of my throne and the place of the soles of my feet, where I will dwell in the midst of the people of Israel forever. And the house of Israel shall no more defile my holy name, neither they, nor their kings, by their whoring and by the dead bodies of their kings at their high places,
